does: This paper introduces DualEval, a latent variable framework that jointly calibrates large language models and evaluation items in a shared representational space. The method simultaneously estimates model ability, item difficulty, and item sharpness (discrimination power) by integrating two previously separate evaluation signals: static benchmark labels with objective correctness and arena-style preference data from reward models validated against human preferences. The authors evaluate 18 frontier LLMs across four diverse domains including coding, mathematics, domain knowledge, and open-ended user queries, demonstrating that DualEval produces reliable and balanced model rankings that outperform disconnected evaluation approaches.
connects: This work bridges a long-standing divide in machine learning evaluation between classical item response theory (IRT) and modern LLM assessment practices. It extends prior IRT frameworks that modeled student ability and test item difficulty to the LLM domain while incorporating recent advances in arena-style evaluation and preference-based model ranking. The approach combines insights from benchmark-based evaluation literature, which established the importance of objective correctness labels, with the emerging preference-learning paradigm popularized by systems like Chatbot Arena. By jointly modeling items and models rather than treating them independently, DualEval synthesizes approaches that had previously operated in isolation.
enables: The unified calibration framework opens several downstream applications and research directions. At the practical level, the learned item-level profiles enable benchmark compression for sample-efficient evaluation and anomaly detection to identify contaminated or outlier items in existing benchmarks. More broadly, the shared latent space provides a principled foundation for better understanding which models excel at different task types and how to design more discriminative evaluation sets. This work points toward more efficient evaluation strategies that could reduce the computational burden of assessing frontier models while improving ranking reliability, and suggests paths toward finer-grained characterization of model strengths and weaknesses across diverse task distributions.
